threats.description: Data that is being stolen or used inappropriately its in this category. The attack can target not only a computer system but also a web service when for instance a session is being hijacked.
exampleThreats: inappropriate use of confidential data in computer system
session hijacking: no method for users to log out of an application / logging out does not clear session states / residual cookies
exploitation of web services where identities are being hijacked & data is being stolen
tampering with URL query strings (modify URL in browser's address bar to expose information to attacker

threats.description: If sensitive data is being inspected apart from the normal usage, the attack belongs to confidential data view.
exampleThreats: inappropriate viewing of data outside the normal usage

threats.description: The illegal distribution of confidential files such as password lists, financial information, and other sensitive material is a part of confidential data transfer.
exampleThreats: National security leaks and other disclosures
illegal distribution & transmission of acquired financial information to outsiders
mail to suspicious addresses (e.g. large number of recipients)

threats.description: Attacks in this category happen when an insider gets access to crypto keys and other credentials without authorization.
exampleThreats: ease of access to a computer system/application entry due to security weaknesses of an older version of an application

threats.description: With data corruption, the fraudulent modification of data can be understood. It happens when information is manipulated within either an application or also a system. Incidents in the past have shown that tampering with cookies is a widely used technique to corrupt data in an unauthorized manner.
exampleThreats: input manipulation (bypass normal data input controls)
computer data manipulation within an application or system
command execution attacks (SQL database calls to change database)
breakage (siphoning off small sums from numerous sources)
attempt to manipulate cookies to 'spoof' server-side authentication mechanism
cookie content manipulation due to absence of an encryption of the cookie

threats.description: In software code programming small modifications can have a huge impact. Logic bombs, trojan horses, and other malicious code injections are examples of this attack group.
exampleThreats: software code modification: 'Logic Bomb' was built into a system to terminate users' sessions and cause a loss of data
software code modification; 'Trojan Horse'  built into a system to terminate user's session & cause loss of data
self-dealing transaction to capitalize on the destruction created by the software code modification
bufferoverflow: attacker can cause web application to execute malicious code that is designed to take over a system
malicious content: spread viruses / Trojan horse programs within valid XML messages
HTML injection (cross-site scripting: use of website to send malicious code

threats.description: The installation of malware can originate from various sources. The use or download of illegal software or offensive material has a higher chance of containing trojan horses or trapdoors in order to compromise a computer system.
exampleThreats: inserting trojan horses or trapdoors in trusted (and untrusted) components

threats.description: When default configurations are being modified or the protection of components gets disabled attackers manipulate system controls.
exampleThreats: inadequate network journaling for forensic purposes

threats.description: All attacks that include hardware are aggregated in this group. Especially when hardware is defective it can get vulnerable to insider attacks. However, an insider is also capable of adding or removing components of hardware to harm a computer system.
exampleThreats: removal / addition of of hardware components

threats.description: In resource exhaustion attacks, the availability of the system is being compromised. Examples that belong to this category are DoS, buffer overflow, and replay attacks.
exampleThreats: damage to system availability (i.e. DDos attack)
coercive parsing: parse a Simple Access Object Protocol message to extract parameters & determine what method to invoke, insert content into database, ... DoS attack.
replay attack: 'ping of death' where repetitive SOAP messages are made to overload web service
denial of service: lack of use of any load testing tools to generate web traffic

threats.description: Unlike resource exhaustion attacks, not the system but the network is not available because of an overload. This can happen when a large amount of data is being downloaded in a small time frame such that the network is not able to process other packets.
exampleThreats: downloading large amount of data in small time period
using over a certain number of network endpoints
using a large network burst (throughput) rate
oversize payloads: transmit a large XML file to deplete CPU resources as a result of parsing & validating each entry.

threats.description: The loss of data because of its destruction by an insider is labeled as data deletion.
exampleThreats: terminate user session in a seemingly random way, causing loss of data
computer data destruction within an application or system

threats.description: There are various ways in which system controls can be circumvented. In the sources, the altering or disabling of audit logs has been mentioned most frequently.
In case of the modification of user access rights, privileges in a system can be elevated. This gives the user the capability to get unauthorized access to information or systems.

Even if users are allowed to access certain data or systems, they can still misuse their privileges to attack an organization. They could for instance abuse an adjustment transaction or error-correction procedures to hide their intrigues.
exampleThreats: misuse of intended authority by over-authorized user
usurpation of superuser access and root keys

threats.description: Attack vectors in social engineering that were found in the context of insider threats are tailgaiting, ingratiation, phishing, pretexting, and baiting. These techniques are applied to deceive an employee in order to gain unauthorized access.
Masquerading as an employee of an enterprise is a typical impersonation attack in insider threats.
exampleThreats: employee impersonation & transmission of unauthorized e-mails to corporate clients
misuse of system's capabilities (impersonation of another insider to send threatening emails to another insider

threats.description: When attackers place themselves in between a client and a server and intercept all the messages that are sent between those two parties, they are called man in the middle.
exampleThreats: man-in-the middle attacks: attacker between client & server, acting as a sever for the client. No SSL used for sites
